---
title: "Concepts & data model"
---

## Points are the primitive

The central design choice of the library is that **all geometry reduces to a set
of points**. A point carries its location in the working coordinate system
(`x`, `y`), its geographic location (`lon`, `lat`), and — where meaningful — a
representative `area` and cell spacing (`dx`, `dy`). Every operation
(distance, projection, neighbor search, interpolation) is defined on points.

A **grid** is then nothing more than points laid out on a structured 2D mesh,
plus axis metadata describing that mesh. This is why the library can
interpolate equally well between two grids, between scattered observations and
a grid, or between polygons and a grid — they are all just points underneath.

```{mermaid}
classDiagram
    class points_class {
      +npts
      +x(:) y(:) lon(:) lat(:)
      +area(:) dx(:) dy(:)
      +mtype, units, planet, proj
    }
    class grid_class {
      +npts
      +x(:,:) y(:,:) lon(:,:) lat(:,:)
      +area(:,:)
      +G : grid_axis_class
    }
    class grid_axis_class {
      +nx ny
      +x0 dx y0 dy
      +x(:) y(:)
    }
    class map_class {
      +npts nmax
      +map(:) : pt_wts_class
    }
    class pt_wts_class {
      +n
      +i(:) dist(:) weight(:) area(:)
      +quadrant(:) border(:)
    }
    grid_class --> grid_axis_class
    grid_class ..> points_class : grid_to_points
    points_class ..> grid_class : points_to_grid
    map_class --> pt_wts_class
```

## The three objects

| Object | Holds | Built by |
|---|---|---|
| `points_class` | 1D vectors of locations (+ optional area, dx/dy) | `points_init` |
| `grid_class` | same, as 2D arrays, plus a `grid_axis_class` | `grid_init` |
| `map_class` | per-target-point neighbor list + weights | `map_init` |

Convert freely between the first two with `grid_to_points(grid, pts)` and
`points_to_grid(pts, grid)`.

### Coordinate-system metadata

Both `points_class` and `grid_class` carry the same descriptive fields:

- `mtype` — the coordinate/map type string: `latlon` / `latitude_longitude` /
  `gaussian`, `cartesian`, or a projection (`stereographic`,
  `polar_stereographic`, `lambert_azimuthal_equal_area`).
- `units` — axis units (`degrees`, `kilometers`, `meters`, …). A scalar
  `xy_conv` factor converts the stored axis units to meters for distance work.
- `planet` — a `planet_class` (either `"WGS84"` or `"Spherical Earth"`).
- `proj` — a `projection_class` with parameters `lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n`
  (only populated when `is_projection`).
- `is_cartesian`, `is_projection`, `is_lon180` — booleans that drive how
  distances and neighbors are computed.

See [Projections & planets](reference/projections.qmd) for the full set of
supported values.

## How a field is mapped

1. **`map_init(map, src, dst, max_neighbors=…)`** finds, for every *target*
   point, its nearest *source* points (up to `max_neighbors`), records their
   indices, distances, quadrants, and inverse-distance weights, and (for
   structured grids) precomputes bilinear coefficients. The whole structure is
   cached to a NetCDF map file keyed by the two grid names and `max_neighbors`.

2. **`map_field(map, name, var_src, var_dst, …, method=…)`** applies those
   stored neighbors/weights to an actual field. The method (`nn`, `bilinear`,
   `quadrant`, `radius`/`shepard`) selects how the per-point neighbor values
   are combined.

Because the neighbor *distances* are stored, the same cached map supports any
distance-based weighting scheme without recomputation — you can change
`method` at `map_field` time for free.

## Distances

Distance is computed two ways, chosen automatically:

- **Cartesian / same projected system** → Euclidean distance in the working
  units (`cartesian_distance`).
- **Otherwise (lon-lat or cross-projection)** → geodesic distance on the
  planet ellipsoid via Karney's
  [GeographicLib](https://geographiclib.sourceforge.io/) algorithms, bundled
  as `geodesic.f90` (`planet_distance`).

This is the rule of thumb stated throughout the codebase: *unless both point
sets live on the same Cartesian/projected system, distances are geodesic
lon-lat calculations.*

## Where the cost is

Two operations dominate runtime, and both are the focus of the
[improvement proposal](improvements.qmd):

- **Neighbor search** in `map_init` is brute force — an O(N×M) double loop over
  source × target points with a latitude pre-filter, no spatial index.
- **Conservative weights** are approximated by dicing each source cell into
  subgrid points and counting how many fall inside the target cell, rather than
  computing the polygon overlap analytically.

Both are correct but slow, which is why heavy conservative remapping is
currently outsourced to the external `cdo` tool (which uses YAC internally).
